question: Define a metric space. List the four axioms that a function d: X × X → ℝ must satisfy to be a metric on a set X.
expectedPoints: A metric space is an ordered pair (X, d) consisting of a set X and a metric (or distance function) d on X.
Non-negativity: For all x, y in X, d(x, y) ≥ 0 and d(x, y) = 0 if and only if x = y.
Symmetry: For all x, y in X, d(x, y) = d(y, x).
Triangle Inequality: For all x, y, z in X, d(x, z) ≤ d(x, y) + d(y, z).

question: Prove that the discrete metric, defined by d(x, y) = 0 if x = y and d(x, y) = 1 if x ≠ y, is indeed a valid metric on any non-empty set X.
expectedPoints: Non-negativity: d(x,y) is either 0 or 1, so it is always ≥ 0. d(x,y)=0 only when x=y, satisfying the first axiom.
Symmetry: The definition is symmetric; the condition for x=y is the same as y=x, and x≠y is the same as y≠x. Therefore d(x,y)=d(y,x).

Triangle Inequality: Consider any x, y, z. If x=z, then d(x,z)=0, which is always ≤ d(x,y)+d(y,z). If x≠z, then d(x,z)=1. For the inequality d(x,z) ≤ d(x,y)+d(y,z) to hold (1 ≤ d(x,y)+d(y,z)), note that if x≠z, then y cannot be equal to both x and z. Therefore, at least one of d(x,y) or d(y,z) must be 1, making their sum at least 1.

question: Let C[0,1] be the set of all real-valued continuous functions on the interval [0,1]. Show that the function d(f, g) = sup{|f(x) - g(x)| : x ∈ [0,1]} defines a metric on C[0,1]. Explain why the supremum is a necessary part of this definition and why the set of continuous functions is a crucial domain for this metric.
expectedPoints: Proof it is a metric: 1) Non-negativity: |f(x)-g(x)|≥0, so the supremum is ≥0. d(f,g)=0 iff f(x)=g(x) for all x, i.e., f=g. 2) Symmetry: |f(x)-g(x)|=|g(x)-f(x)|, so sup is the same. 3) Triangle Inequality: For any f,g,h, |f(x)-g(x)| ≤ |f(x)-h(x)| + |h(x)-g(x)| ≤ d(f,h) + d(h,g) for all x. Therefore, d(f,h)+d(h,g) is an upper bound for the set {|f(x)-g(x)|}, so the supremum d(f,g) must be ≤ d(f,h)+d(h,g).

Necessity of Supremum: The supremum captures the maximum separation between the two functions over the entire interval. Using the integral or another measure would not capture the concept of uniform distance, which is central to uniform convergence.

Importance of Continuous Functions: The Extreme Value Theorem guarantees that for continuous functions f and g on [0,1], |f-g| is also continuous on the closed interval [0,1] and therefore attains a maximum value. This ensures the supremum is actually a maximum and is finite, making d(f,g) a well-defined real number.
